what_it_does: Records a grounding-quality gap where many corpus records carry a non-string output, tracks it as a separate sub-item of a rerank investigation, documents the resulting rerank skip behavior, a read-side mitigation, and a remaining write-side normalization recommendation.
observations: 13 of 24 candidates in a measured decision pool had a non-string output (a list, not a string/dict)
runtime/corpus_rerank.py:_digest_text returns the raw non-string and rerank_hits skips it with isinstance(text, str)
The sharper re-sort therefore only saw 11 of 24 candidates
The preferred root fix is normalizing output to readable text at corpus write time, or teaching _digest_text to render lists
Status is non-blocking while the sharper sort is off, but becomes load-bearing if the sharper sort is restored
UPDATE 2026-06-18: read-side handled via _clean_meaning rendering both structured dicts and lists of insight strings; coverage improved from 5/10 to 10/10 with hot-path budget ~1.9s
What remains is a consistency nicety: corpus write/digest still emits heterogeneous output shapes (dict, list, string)
The rerank path still json.dumps non-string output as cross-encoder input
standouts: Read-side mitigation already closed the immediate grounding-legibility gap
Write-side normalization remains optional but would simplify every reader and the rerank path
The issue is load-bearing only when the sharper rerank sort is turned back on
conventions: Uses a structured gap/incident format with What, Why it matters, Root, Status, and Update sections
References board items and decisions with URI-like identifiers such as board://item-a3844c46 and decision://global/rerank-loadout
Distinguishes read-side fixes from write-side fixes and assigns ownership
Uses bold for key metrics and timestamps for updates
concerns: Heterogeneous output shapes persisted in the corpus may surprise or burden future readers
The rerank path still feeds non-string output through json.dumps as cross-encoder input
The non-blocking classification depends on the sharper sort remaining off; restoring it without the write-side fix would reintroduce the gap
notes: A gap note tracking that half of corpus candidates had non-string outputs invisible to rerank, now mitigated read-side but still worth normalizing at write time.
questions: What exact schema does the corpus write/digest pass use today?
Which component or team owns corpus-capture/digest normalization?
What would be the decision-surface impact if the sharper sort is restored before the write-side fix is done?
What do RHM and territory_prose refer to in this context?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file states it is a separate sub-item tracking a grounding-quality gap, and its content documents the gap, root cause, status, and read-side resolution while leaving write-side normalization as a follow-up.
apparent_themes: Grounding quality and corpus data hygiene
intention_signals: Surface a hidden quality ceiling in rerank precision caused by heterogeneous output shapes, close the immediate grounding risk with a read-side renderer, and steer the durable fix toward corpus-write normalization.
novelty: Explicitly separates the rerank loadout decision (whether the sharper sort runs) from the data-shape gap (what the sharper sort can read), treating them as independent concerns.
contribution: Captures a concrete measurement (13/24 non-string outputs) and a two-phase resolution path (read-side mitigation plus write-side normalization recommendation), preserving the rationale for why corpus output shape matters to both grounding and rerank.
summary_for_embedding: A gap note in the rerank investigation records that 13 of 24 corpus candidates had non-string outputs, causing the sharper rerank to skip them, documents a read-side fix that restored full candidate coverage, and leaves corpus-write normalization as a remaining consistency task.
intention_for_embedding: To track a grounding-quality ceiling caused by heterogeneous corpus output shapes and guide the remaining write-side schema normalization.
